abstract:
INTRODUCTION: Trials demonstrate significant clinical benefit in patients receiving therapeutic hypothermia (TH) after cardiac arrest. However, incidence of mortality and morbidity remains high in this patient group. Rapid targeted brain hypothermia induction, together with prompt correction of the underlying cause may improve outcomes in these patients. This study investigates the efficacy of Rhinochill, an intranasal cooling device over Blanketrol, a surface cooling device in inducing TH in cardiac arrest patients within the cardiac catheter laboratory.

METHODS: 70 patients were randomized to TH induction with either Rhinochill or Blanketrol. Primary outcome measures were time to reach tympanic в‰¤34 В°C from randomisation as a surrogate for brain temperature and oesophageal в‰¤34 В°C from randomisation as a measurement of core body temperature. Secondary outcomes included first hour temperature drop, length of stay in intensive care unit, hospital stay, neurological recovery and all-cause mortality at hospital discharge.

RESULTS: There was no difference in time to reach в‰¤34 В°C between Rhinochill and Blanketrol (Tympanic в‰¤34 В°C, 75 vs. 107 mins; p=0.101; Oesophageal в‰¤34 В°C, 85 vs. 115 mins; p=0.151). Tympanic temperature dropped significantly with Rhinochill in the first hour (1.75 vs. 0.94 В°C; p<0.001). No difference was detected in any other secondary outcome measures. Catheter laboratory-based TH induction resulted in a survival to hospital discharge of 67.1%.

CONCLUSION: In this study, Rhinochill was not found to be more efficient than Blanketrol for TH induction, although there was a non-significant trend in favour of Rhinochill that potentially warrants further investigation with a larger trial.

Title: A Randomized Trial Among Compression Plus Nonsteroidal Antiinflammatory Drugs, Aspiration, and Aspiration With Steroid Injection for Nonseptic Olecranon Bursitis.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: "Trend toward significance" // "Numerically longer survival" or equivalent verbiage, Claim benefit based on (+) secondary endpoint
abstract:
Background: Olecranon bursitis is a relatively common condition encountered in outpatient clinics. There are few well-designed studies comparing approaches to treatment.

Questions/purposes: (1) Which treatment (compression bandaging with nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs [NSAIDs], aspiration, or aspiration with steroid injections) is associated with the highest likelihood of resolution of nonseptic olecranon bursitis?

Methods: We enrolled 133 patients from two centers; after applying prespecified exclusions (septic bursitis or concomitant inflammatory arthritis, intraarticular elbow pathology, recent aspiration or steroid injection done elsewhere, and refusal to participate), 90 patients were randomly allocated to receive compression bandaging with NSAIDs (C), aspiration (A), or aspiration with steroid injection (AS) groups (30 patients in each). The groups were similar at baseline in terms of age and gender. Seven patients (four from Group A and three from Group AS) were lost to follow-up. All patients were followed up weekly for 4 weeks, and the same treatment procedure was repeated if the bursitis recurred with any substantial fluid collection. At 4 weeks, the state of resolution was evaluated. Failed resolution was defined as the presence of persistent olecranon bursal fluid collection at Week 4 after the initiation of the treatment; if bursal fluid collection was clinically reduced or completely disappeared by the end of Week 4, the treatment was considered successful. We compared the proportion of resolution by Week 4 among the treatment groups.

Results: There were no differences in the proportion of patients whose bursitis resolved by Week 4 among the three treatment groups (Group C: 25 of 30 [83%], relative risk of resolution failure: 0.68 [95% confidence interval {CI}, 0.27-1.72], p = 0.580; Group A: 17 of 26 [65%], relative risk of resolution failure: 2.19 [95% CI, 0.98-4.87], p = 0.083; Group AS: 23 of 27 [85%], relative risk of resolution failure: 0.59 [95% CI, 0.22-1.63], p = 0.398) (p = 0.073).

Conclusions: The treatment with compression bandaging with NSAIDs, aspiration, and aspiration with steroid injection showed no significant differences in efficacy for the resolution of nonseptic olecranon bursitis by Week 4.

1. Wearing a special bandage that squeezes the elbow, along with taking medicine to reduce pain and swelling.
2. Using a needle to drain out the extra fluid that's causing the swelling.
3. Using a needle to drain out the extra fluid, and also injecting a special medicine to help reduce swelling.

The researchers looked at 90 people who had this condition, and they divided them into three groups to try out each of the three treatments. They checked on the people every week for four weeks to see if the swelling was getting better.

What they found out was that all three treatments worked about the same. About 65-85% of the people in each group got better by the end of four weeks. This means that there wasn't one treatment that stood out as being much better than the others.

So, the main conclusion is that doctors can use any of these three treatments to help people with olecranon bursitis, and they'll all work about equally well.

Title: A Randomized Trial Among Compression Plus Nonsteroidal Antiinflammatory Drugs, Aspiration, and Aspiration With Steroid Injection for Nonseptic Olecranon Bursitis.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: "Trend toward significance" // "Numerically longer survival" or equivalent verbiage, Claim benefit based on (+) secondary endpoint
abstract:
Background: Olecranon bursitis might be a minor problem in the outpatient clinic but relatively be common to occur. However, there are few well-designed studies comparing approaches to treatment.

Questions/purposes: (1) Which treatment (compression bandaging with nonsteroidal antiinflammatory drugs [NSAIDs], aspiration, or aspiration with steroid injections) is associated with the highest likelihood of resolution of nonseptic olecranon bursitis? (2) Which treatment is associated with earliest resolution of symptoms? (3) What factors are associated with treatment failure by 4 weeks?

Methods: We enrolled 133 patients from two centers; after applying prespecified exclusions (septic bursitis or concomitant inflammatory arthritis, intraarticular elbow pathology, recent aspiration or steroid injection done elsewhere, and refusal to participate), 90 patients were randomly allocated to receive compression bandaging with NSAIDs (C), aspiration (A), or aspiration with steroid injection (AS) groups (30 patients in each). The groups were similar at baseline in terms of age and gender. Seven patients (four from Group A and three from Group AS) were lost to followup. All patients were followed up weekly for 4 weeks, and the same treatment procedure was repeated if the bursitis recurred with any substantial fluid collection. At 4 weeks, the state of resolution and pain visual analog scale (VAS) were evaluated. Failed resolution was defined as presence of persistent olecranon bursal fluid collection at Week 4 after the initiation of the treatment; on the contrary, if bursal fluid collection was clinically reduced or completely disappeared by the end of Week 4, the treatment was considered successful. We compared the proportion of resolution by Week 4 and the median times to resolution among the treatment groups. In addition, we evaluated whether the resolution affected pain VAS and what factors were associated with the resolution.

Results: There were no differences in the proportion of patients whose bursitis resolved by Week 4 among the three treatment groups (Group C: 25 of 30 [83%], relative risk of resolution failure: 0.68 [95% confidence interval {CI}, 0.27-1.72], p = 0.580; Group A: 17 of 26 [65%], relative risk of resolution failure: 2.19 [95% CI, 0.98-4.87], p = 0.083; Group AS: 23 of 27 [85%], relative risk of resolution failure: 0.59 [95% CI, 0.22-1.63], p = 0.398) (p = 0.073). Steroid injection after aspiration (Group AS) was associated with the earliest resolution (2.3 weeks [range, 1-4 weeks]) when compared with aspiration alone (Group A; 3.1 weeks [range, 2-4 weeks]) and compression bandaging with NSAIDs (Group C; 3.2 weeks [range, 2-4 weeks]), p = 0.015). Longer duration of symptoms before treatment was the only factor associated with treatment failure by 4 weeks (failed resolution: 6 weeks [range, 2-9 weeks]; successful resolution: 4 weeks [range, 0.4-6 weeks]; p = 0.008).

Conclusions: With the numbers available, there were no differences in efficacy when compression bandaging with NSAIDs, aspiration, and aspiration with steroid injection were compared. However, we were powered only to detect a 30% difference, meaning that if there were a smaller difference in efficacy among the groups, we might not have detected it in a study of this size. Our data can be used as pilot data to power future prospective (and likely multicenter) trials. Because olecranon bursitis can recur, and because treatments like aspiration and aspiration with steroid injection can cause complications, unless future trials demonstrate clear efficacy advantages of aspiration and/or injection both at short and longer terms, we suggest that compression bandaging and a short course of NSAIDs may offer the most appropriate balance of safety and efficacy.

Title: Radiological outcomes in a randomized trial comparing opening wedge and closing wedge techniques of high tibial osteotomy.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Other
abstract:
Purpose: The aim of the present study was to examine changes in radiological variables in a prospective randomized study comparing opening wedge (OW) and closing wedge (CW) techniques of high tibial osteotomy (HTO). Our hypothesis was that there would be no differences in joint line angles or correction accuracy between the two groups, that patellar height would increase after CW HTO and decrease after OW HTO, and that leg length and posterior tibial slope would decrease after CW HTO and increase after OW HTO.

Methods: Radiological data were collected from 70 patients participating in an ongoing prospective randomized clinical trial comparing OW and CW HTOs. Digital standing hip-knee-ankle (HKA) radiographs as well as lateral radiographs in 30Ў of flexion were obtained preoperatively and at 6 months for each patient. Joint line angles, HKA angle, leg length, Insall-Salvati index, Miura-Kawamura index and posterior tibial slope were measured using medical planning software. The complete preoperative radiological examinations of the first 50 patients were used in a study of intra- and inter-rater reliability of the measurements.

Results: The mean posterior slope was reduced by 2.5Ў in CW HTO, whereas it remained unchanged in OW HTO (p < 0.001). Mean leg length decreased 5.7 mm in CW HTO and increased 3.1 mm in OW HTO (p < 0.001). Changes in joint line angles, patellar height indexes and the correction accuracy showed no significant differences comparing the two techniques. Frontal plane reliability measurement intra- and inter-rater intraclass correlation coefficient (ICC) varied from 0.81 to 0.99. Sagittal plane intra- and inter-rater ICC varied from 0.60 to 0.87. Posterior tibial slope intra- and inter-rater ICC showed the lowest values (0.70 and 0.60, respectively) corresponding to a smallest real difference of 4.5Ў and 5.5Ў, respectively.

Conclusions: Posterior tibial slope and leg length changes were significantly different in CW compared to OW HTOs. We recommend that possible alterations in tibial slope and leg length are considered when the technique of HTO is to be chosen. Landmark-based medical planning software shows good reliability and can be used in preoperative planning and postoperative evaluations of HTOs.

Title: Emergency Department Management of Sepsis Patients: A Randomized, Goal-Oriented, Noninvasive Sepsis Trial.
abstract:
STUDY OBJECTIVE: We examine whether using a noninvasive protocol would result in more rapid lactate clearance after 3 hours in patients with severe sepsis and septic shock in the emergency department.

METHODS: In this open-label randomized controlled trial, 122 adult patients with sepsis and serum lactate concentration of greater than or equal to 3.0 mmol/L were randomized to receive usual care or intravenous fluid bolus administration guided by measurements of change of stroke volume index, using the noninvasive cardiac output monitor after passive leg-raising maneuver. The primary outcome was lactate clearance of more than 20% at 3 hours. Analysis was intention to treat.

RESULTS: Similar proportions of patients in the randomized intervention group (70.5%; N=61) versus control group (73.8%; N=61) achieved the primary outcome, with a relative risk of 0.96 (95% confidence interval [CI] 0.77 to 1.19). Hospital mortality occurred in 6 patients (9.8%) each in the intervention and control groups on or before 28 days (relative risk=1.00; 95% CI 0.34 to 2.93).

CONCLUSION: The treatment using the noninvasive cardiac output monitor and passive leg-raising maneuver was not more effective than usual care in patients with severe sepsis and septic shock.

2. A new way that uses a special machine to help the doctors give the right amount of fluid to the patient (this is called the "noninvasive protocol").

Title: Emergency Department Management of Sepsis Patients: A Randomized, Goal-Oriented, Noninvasive Sepsis Trial.

We examine whether using a noninvasive protocol would result in more rapid lactate clearance after 3 hours in patients with severe sepsis and septic shock in the emergency department.

METHODS: In this open-label randomized controlled trial, 122 adult patients with sepsis and serum lactate concentration of greater than or equal to 3.0 mmol/L were randomized to receive usual care or intravenous fluid bolus administration guided by measurements of change of stroke volume index, using the noninvasive cardiac output monitor after passiveВ leg-raising maneuver. The primary outcome was lactate clearance of more than 20% at 3 hours. Secondary outcomes included mortality, length of hospital and ICU stay, and total hospital cost. Analysis was intention to treat.

RESULTS: Similar proportions of patients in the randomized intervention group (70.5%; N=61) versus control group (73.8%; N=61) achieved the primary outcome, with a relative risk of 0.96 (95% confidence interval [CI] 0.77 to 1.19). Secondary outcomes were similar in both groups (P>.05 for all comparisons). Hospital mortality occurred in 6 patients (9.8%) each in the intervention and control groups on or before 28 days (relative risk=1.00; 95% CI 0.34 to 2.93). Among a subgroup of patients with underlying fluid overload states, those in the intervention group tended to receive clinically significantly more intravenous fluids at 3 hours (difference=975 mL; 95% CI -450 to 1,725 mL) and attained better lactate clearance (difference=19.7%; 95% CI -34.6% to 60.2%) compared with the control group, with shorter hospital lengths of stay (difference=-4.5 days; 95% CI -9.5 to 2.5 days).

CONCLUSION: Protocol-based fluid resuscitation of patients with severe sepsis and septic shock with the noninvasive cardiac output monitor and passive leg-raising maneuver did not result in better outcomes compared with usual care. Future studies to demonstrate the use of the noninvasive protocol-based care in patients with preexisting fluid overload states may be warranted.

I'd be happy to help you explain this passage to your fifth grader.

This passage is about a medical study that tried to find a better way to help people who are very sick with an infection called sepsis. Sepsis can make people's bodies not work properly, and it can be life-threatening.

The study wanted to see if using a special machine that can measure how well a person's heart is working, without having to insert any tubes or needles into their body, could help doctors give the right amount of fluid to these sick people. The machine is called a "noninvasive cardiac output monitor," and it's used along with a simple movement called a "passive leg-raising maneuver" (which is just a fancy way of saying "lifting their legs up").

The doctors thought that using this machine might help them give the right amount of fluid to the patients, which would make them get better faster. They compared two groups of patients: one group got the usual care, and the other group got the special machine and fluid treatment.

But, after looking at the results, the doctors found out that using the special machine didn't make a big difference in how well the patients got better. Most patients in both groups got better at about the same rate.

However, the doctors did notice that the machine might be helpful for a specific group of patients who already had too much fluid in their bodies. These patients might benefit from using the machine to get the right amount of fluid

Title: Combined Administration of Systemic and Topical Tranexamic Acid for Total Knee Arthroplasty: Can It Be a Better Regimen and Yet Safe? A Randomized Controlled Trial.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Other
abstract:
Background:В Total knee arthroplasty (TKA) is associated with substantial blood loss in postoperative period. Tranexamic acid (TXA) is potent antifibrinolytic agent, routinely administered by intravenous (IV) and topical route, which can possibly interrupt cascade of events due to hemostatic irregularities close to source of bleeding. However, scientific evidence of combined administration of TXA in TKA is still meagre. The present study aimed to compare efficacy of combined IV and topical TXA with IV use alone in terms of blood loss, transfusion rate, and incidence of deep vein thrombosis and thromboembolism. 
Patients and methods:В 119 patients undergoing unilateral TKA were randomized into IV alone and combined group. Patients assigned to IV group were given IV TXA as a preoperative and postoperative dose given 3 and 6 hours after surgery, whereas in combined group, topical TXA solution was applied intraarticularly about 5 minutes before closure of arthrotomy in addition to IV doses. 
Results:В Combined use of IV and topical TXA provided better results than IV use alone with mean calculated total blood loss (590.69В±191.1 vs 385.68В±182.5, P<.001), blood transfusion rate (6.6% vs 1.6%, P = .364), hemoglobin drop (1.82В±0.6 vs 1.14В±0.5, P<.001). No case of DVT or TE was noted among the 2 study groups. 
Conclusion:В Combined use of IV and intraarticular TXA provided significantly better results compared with IV use alone with respect to all variables related to postoperative blood loss in TKA. Moreover, TXA use is safe in terms of incidence of symptomatic DVT and TE.
plain_language_summary:
I'd be happy to help you explain this passage to your fifth grader.

So, this passage is talking about a medical study that looked at how to reduce bleeding after a certain kind of surgery called a total knee replacement (TKA). This surgery helps people who have very bad knees, but it can cause a lot of bleeding afterwards.

The doctors were testing a special medicine called tranexamic acid (TXA) to see if it could help reduce the bleeding. They wanted to know if giving the medicine in two ways (through a vein and directly into the knee) was better than just giving it through a vein.

They did an experiment with 119 patients who were having this surgery. They divided the patients into two groups: one group got the medicine only through a vein, and the other group got it through a vein and also directly into the knee.

The results showed that the group that got the medicine in both ways had less bleeding, needed fewer blood transfusions, and had less of a drop in their hemoglobin (which is a measure of how much oxygen is in their blood). And the best part is that none of the patients in either group got a bad blood clot or other complications.

So, the conclusion is that giving the medicine in both ways (through a vein and directly into the knee) is better than just giving it through a vein, and it's also safe.

I hope that helps! Let me know if you or your fifth grader have any further questions.

Title: Combined Administration of Systemic and Topical Tranexamic Acid for Total Knee Arthroplasty: Can It Be a Better Regimen and Yet Safe? A Randomized Controlled Trial.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Other
abstract:
Background: Total knee arthroplasty (TKA) is associated with substantial blood loss in the postoperative period. Tranexamic acid (TXA) is a potent antifibrinolytic agent, routinely administered by intravenous (IV) and topical routes. The present study aimed to compare the efficacy of combined IV and topical TXA with IV use alone in terms of blood loss.

Patients and methods: 119 patients undergoing unilateral TKA were randomized into IV alone and combined groups. Patients assigned to the IV group were given IV TXA as a preoperative and postoperative dose given 3 and 6 hours after surgery, whereas in the combined group, topical TXA solution was applied intraarticularly about 5 minutes before closure of arthrotomy in addition to IV doses.

Results: The mean calculated total blood loss was 590.69±191.1 mL in the IV group and 385.68±182.5 mL in the combined group. The blood transfusion rate was 6.6% in the IV group and 1.6% in the combined group. The hemoglobin drop was 1.82±0.6 g/dL in the IV group and 1.14±0.5 g/dL in the combined group. No case of deep vein thrombosis (DVT) or thromboembolism (TE) was noted among the two study groups.

Conclusion: The treatment with combined IV and intraarticular TXA was not more effective than IV use alone in patients with TKA in terms of blood loss.

Here's a summary of the passage that a fifth grader can understand:

The researchers in this study wanted to see if giving TXA both through a vein and directly to the knee would be better than just giving it through a vein. They looked at two groups of patients: one group got TXA only through a vein, and the other group got it both through a vein and directly to the knee.

What they found out was that the group that got TXA both ways didn't lose less blood than the group that only got it through a vein. They also didn't need as many blood transfusions. But, the good news is that neither group had any serious problems with blood clots.

So, the researchers concluded that giving TXA both ways isn't better than just giving it through a vein when it comes to reducing blood loss after knee surgery.

Title: Physical activity intensity and type 2 diabetes risk in overweight youth: a randomized trial.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Other;Claim benefit based on (+) secondary endpoint
abstract:
Background: The chronic effects of high-intensity endurance training on metabolic health outcomes in overweight adolescents remain poorly understood.

Objective: To test the hypothesis that high-intensity endurance training (ET) is superior to moderate-intensity ET for improving risk factors for type 2 diabetes in overweight adolescents.

Design and methods: In this randomized trial, 106 overweight and obese adolescents (15.2 years; 76% female; 62% Caucasian) were randomly assigned to high-intensity ET (70-85% of heart rate reserve, n=38), moderate-intensity ET (40-55% heart rate reserve; n=32), or control for 6 months (n=36). The primary outcome measure was insulin sensitivity assessed using a frequently sampled intravenous glucose tolerance test. Secondary outcome measures included hepatic triglyceride content with magnetic resonance spectroscopy.

Results: The study had 96% retention, and attendance was 61±21% and 55±24% in the high- and moderate-intensity ET arms. Intention-to-treat analyses revealed that, at follow-up, insulin sensitivity was not different between high-intensity (-1.0 mU kg(-1) min(-1); 95% confidence interval (CI): -1.6, +1.4 mU kg(-1) min(-1)) and moderate-intensity (+0.26 mU kg(-1) min(-1); 95% CI: -1.3, +1.8 mU kg(-1) min(-1)) ET arms compared with controls (interaction, P=0.97). Similarly, hepatic triglyceride at follow-up was not different in high-intensity (-1.7% fat/water (F/W); 95% CI: -7.0, +3.6% F/W) and moderate-intensity (-0.40% F/W; 95% CI: -6.0, +5.3% F/W) ET compared with controls.

Conclusions: The treatment of high-intensity endurance training was not more effective than moderate-intensity endurance training on insulin sensitivity in overweight adolescents.
plain_language_summary:
I'd be happy to help you explain this passage to your fifth grader.

So, the researchers were trying to figure out if a certain type of exercise could help kids who are overweight or obese (meaning they weigh more than they should for their age and height) be healthier.

They wanted to know if doing really hard exercise (like running really fast) for a long time would be better than doing exercise that's not as hard (like jogging or walking fast) for a long time. They were looking at how this exercise would affect the kids' bodies, especially when it comes to a disease called type 2 diabetes.

They took 106 kids who were overweight or obese and divided them into three groups. One group did really hard exercise, one group did not-as-hard exercise, and the third group didn't do any special exercise at all. They did this for six months.

After six months, they tested the kids to see if the exercise had helped them. But what they found out was that it didn't matter if the kids did really hard exercise or not-as-hard exercise - both groups were about the same when it came to being healthy. The exercise didn't seem to make a big difference.

So, the researchers concluded that doing really hard exercise isn't necessarily better than doing not-as-hard exercise when it comes to helping kids who are overweight or obese be healthier.

Does that make sense?

Title: Physical activity intensity and type 2 diabetes risk in overweight youth: a randomized trial.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Other;Claim benefit based on (+) secondary endpoint
abstract:
Background: The chronic effects of high-intensity endurance training on metabolic health outcomes in overweight adolescents remains poorly understood.

Objective: To test the hypothesis that high-intensity endurance training (ET) is superior to moderate-intensity ET for improving risk factors for type 2 diabetes in overweight adolescents.

Design and methods: In this randomized trial, 106 overweight and obese adolescents (15.2 years; 76% female; 62% Caucasian) were randomly assigned to high-intensity ET (70-85% of heart rate reserve, n=38), moderate-intensity ET (40-55% heart rate reserve; n=32) or control for 6 months (n=36). The primary and secondary outcome measures were insulin sensitivity assessed using a frequently sampled intravenous glucose tolerance test and hepatic triglyceride content with magnetic resonance spectroscopy. Exploratory outcomes were cardiorespiratory fitness, physical activity and MRI and dual x-ray absorptiometry-derived measures of adiposity.

Results: The study had 96% retention and attendance was 61±21% and 55±24% in the high- and moderate-intensity ET arms. Intention-to-treat analyses revealed that, at follow-up, insulin sensitivity was not different between high-intensity (-1.0 mU kg(-1) min(-1); 95% confidence interval (CI): -1.6, +1.4 mU kg(-1) min(-1)) and moderate-intensity (+0.26 mU kg(-1) min(-1); 95% CI: -1.3, +1.8 mU kg(-1) min(-1)) ET arms compared with controls (interaction, P=0.97). Similarly, hepatic triglyceride at follow-up was not different in high-intensity (-1.7% fat/water (F/W); 95% CI: -7.0, +3.6% F/W) and moderate-intensity (-0.40% FW; 95% CI: -6.0, +5.3% F/W) ET compared with controls. Both high intensity (+4.4 ml per kg-FFM (fat-free mass) per minute; 95% CI: 1.7, 7.1 ml kg-FFM(-1) min(-1)) and moderate intensity (+4.4 ml kg-FFM(-1) min(-1); 95% CI: 1.6, 7.3 ml kg-FFM(-1) min(-1)) increased cardiorespiratory fitness, relative to controls (interaction P<0.001).

Conclusions: ET improves cardiorespiratory fitness among obese adolescents; however, owing to lack of compliance, the influence of exercise intensity on insulin sensitivity and hepatic triglycerides remains unclear.
plain_language_summary:
Here's a summary of the passage that a fifth grader can understand:

The researchers wanted to know if exercising really hard (high-intensity) or exercising at a moderate pace (not too hard, not too easy) would help overweight teenagers be healthier. They looked at 106 teenagers who were overweight or obese and divided them into three groups: one group exercised really hard, one group exercised at a moderate pace, and one group didn't exercise at all (the control group).

The researchers wanted to see if exercising would help the teenagers in two main ways: by making their bodies better at using insulin (a hormone that helps us use sugar for energy) and by reducing the amount of fat in their livers. They also looked at how well the teenagers could exercise (their "cardiorespiratory fitness") and how much fat they had in their bodies.

After six months, the researchers found that both the high-intensity and moderate-intensity exercise groups got better at exercising, but they didn't find any difference between the two groups in terms of how well their bodies used insulin or how much fat was in their livers. This was partly because not all the teenagers stuck to their exercise plans, which made it hard to get accurate results.

So, the main takeaway is that exercising (whether it's high-intensity or moderate-intensity) can help overweight teenagers get better at exercising, but the researchers need to do more studies to figure out if it really helps with insulin and liver fat.

Title: A Randomized Trial Comparing Ceramic-on-Ceramic Bearing vs Ceramic-on-Crossfire-Polyethylene Bearing Surfaces in Total Hip Arthroplasty.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Other
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Bearing surfaces in total hip arthroplasty (THA) may affect implant longevity and hence patient outcomes. This randomized clinical trial determined how ceramic-on-ceramic (CERAMIC) bearing THA affected joint-specific pain, function, and stiffness and prosthesis fixation/longevity over 10 postoperative years compared with ceramic-on-highly-crosslinked-polyethylene (POLYETHYLENE) bearing THA. This is a follow-up to previously reported 5-year outcomes.

METHODS: Subjects aged <61 years were randomized to CERAMIC (nВ = 48) or POLYETHYLENE (nВ = 44) THA. Subjects were assessed using the Western Ontario McMaster Osteoarthritis Index and the RAND 12-Item Health Survey preoperatively, and at 1, 5, and 10 years postoperatively. Plain radiographs were evaluated at 10 years for fixation, and medical records were reviewed for revisions.

RESULTS: Of 92 subjects, 6 (7%) died within 10 years; 68 (79%) survivors provided radiographic and/or clinical follow-up at 10 years postoperatively. Improvements seen at 5 years in both the Western Ontario McMaster Osteoarthritis Index and RAND 12-Item Health Survey were retained at 10 years with no group differences (P > .48). There were no failures or loss of fixation related to bearing surfaces/wear in either group. Over 10 years, 3 subjects in the POLYETHYLENE group had revisions that were related to recurrent dislocation; 2 revisions were performed within 2 years of surgery, and one further subject underwent revision at 7 years postoperatively.CONCLUSIONS: This is one of the first randomized clinical trials to examine 10-year outcomes between CERAMIC and POLYETHYLENE bearing THAs. Both bearing surfaces performed well out to 10 years in subjects who were <61 years at time of surgery.
plain_language_summary:
I'd be happy to help you explain this passage to your fifth grader.

So, this passage is talking about a special kind of surgery called a "total hip arthroplasty" or THA for short. It's when doctors replace a person's hip joint with an artificial one.

The researchers wanted to know which type of artificial hip joint is better: one made with ceramic parts (like a plate and a ball) or one made with a ceramic plate and a polyethylene (a type of plastic) ball. They wanted to see which one works better and lasts longer.

They did an experiment where they randomly chose 92 people to get one of the two types of artificial hip joints. Then, they checked in with these people over the next 10 years to see how they were doing.

What they found out was that both types of artificial hip joints worked well and helped people feel better. Most people didn't have any problems with their new hip joints, and they were able to move around and do things without too much pain.

Only a few people had some issues, like their hip joint coming loose or dislocating (which means the ball comes out of the socket). But these problems were not because of the type of artificial hip joint they had.

So, the researchers concluded that both types of artificial hip joints are good options for people who need this kind of surgery, especially if they're under 61 years old.

Does that make sense?

Title: Rotator cuff healing after needling of a calcific deposit using platelet-rich plasma augmentation: a randomized, prospective clinical trial.
Journal: Journal of Shoulder and Elbow Surgery
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Other
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Arthroscopic needling of a rotator cuff calcification is a highly reliable operation in terms of pain relief and return of function. However, during the needling process, a cuff defect is created. Little is known about the evolution of this defect.

METHODS: We conducted a prospective, randomized controlled clinical trial to investigate the evolution of the aforementioned defect and the role of platelet-rich plasma (PRP) augmentation in this healing process. Patients were randomized to either group 1 (PRP, nвЂ‰=вЂ‰20) or group 2 (no PRP [control group], nвЂ‰=вЂ‰20). Patients in group 1 received a perioperative PRP infiltration at the rotator cuff defect, whereas the control group did not. Patients were assessed clinically preoperatively and postoperatively at 6 weeks, 3 and 6 months, and 1 year. The Constant score, Simple Shoulder Test, and QuickDASH (short version of Disabilities of the Arm, Shoulder and Hand questionnaire) were used as outcome measures. The evolution of the cuff defect was evaluated on sonography at 3 and 6 months and with magnetic resonance imaging after 1 year.

RESULTS: All patients improved significantly after surgery (PвЂ‰<вЂ‰.05). There was no difference in clinical outcome or rotator cuff healing between groups. We observed a high rate of persistent rotator cuff defects after 1 year in both groups. The presence of residual cuff defects did not influence the clinical outcome.

CONCLUSION: Arthroscopic needling is an operation with a predictive, good clinical outcome. We found a high rate of persistent rotator cuff defects after 1 year. This study could not identify any beneficial effect of the addition of PRP on rotator cuff healing.

Title: Single vs Repeat Surgical Skin Preparations for Reducing Surgical Site Infection After Total Joint Arthroplasty: A Prospective, Randomized, Double-Blinded Study.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Other
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Preventing surgical site infection (SSI) after total joint arthroplasty (TJA) is a high priority and is partly linked to the efficacy of surgical site preparation solutions (SPSs) in reducing the number of pathogens on the skin before incision. The aim of this study is to investigate the effectiveness of SRS reapplication after draping to reduce the incidence of SSI after TJA.

METHODS: Six hundred patients undergoing primary TJA between 2010 and 2011 at a single institution were recruited and randomly assigned to one of 2 groups. The patients in the intervention group (nВ = 300) received SPS that included alcohol and povidone-iodine before draping and an additional SPS by iodine povacrylex and isopropyl alcohol before application of the final adhesive drape, whereas the patients in the control group (nВ = 300) received a single SPS with alcohol and povidone-iodine before draping. Randomization was performed by an opaque envelope, and the rates of SSI and blistering were compared between groups.

RESULTS: Five seventy-seven patients completed the study and were included in the final analysis. There was a significant reduction in the incidence of superficial SSI for the intervention group (1.8%, 5 of 283) compared to the control group (6.5%, 19 of 294, PВ = .02). There were 2 (0.7%, 2 of 294) deep incisional SSIs in the control group, and 2 (0.7%, 2 of 283) organ-space SSIs in the intervention group (PВ = 1.00). In addition, skin blistering was lower in the intervention group (3.5%, 10 of 283) vs the control group (6.5%, 19 of 294), but this difference also did not reach statistical significance (PВ = .13).

CONCLUSION: Reapplication of an SPS after draping and before the application of iodophor-impregnated incisive draping resulted in a significant reduction in the rate of SSI in patients undergoing elective TJA.

I'd be happy to help you explain this passage to your fifth grader.

So, when people have surgery to replace a joint (like a hip or knee), doctors want to make sure they don't get an infection afterwards. To help prevent infections, doctors use special solutions to clean the skin before making the incision (cut).

This study was trying to figure out if using an extra layer of cleaning solution after the patient is all draped up (covered with a special sheet) would help prevent infections even more.

They tested this by dividing 600 patients into two groups. One group got the extra cleaning solution, and the other group didn't. Then, they compared how many people in each group got infections.

What they found out was that the group that got the extra cleaning solution had fewer infections (only 1.8% of them got infected, compared to 6.5% of the other group). They also found that the group with the extra cleaning solution had fewer blisters on their skin.

So, the conclusion is that using an extra layer of cleaning solution after the patient is all draped up can help prevent infections after joint replacement surgery.

Title: Double-Row Repair Lowers the Retear Risk After Accelerated Rehabilitation.
Journal: The American Journal of Sports Medicine

abstract:
BACKGROUND: Recently, an accelerated rehabilitation protocol after rotator cuff (RC) repair has been proposed for patients at risk of postoperative stiffness.

PURPOSE: To investigate whether double-row (DR) repair provides better clinical outcomes and a lower retear rate compared with single-row (SR) configurations in patients undergoing early accelerated mobilization.

STUDY DESIGN: Randomized controlled trial; Level of evidence, 1.

METHODS: A total of 58 patients were randomized to undergo either SR or DR repair. After 2 years of follow-up, 12 men and 13 women (mean age, 61.8 years; range, 52-67 years) in the SR group and 15 men and 10 women (mean age, 58.9 years; range, 51-69 years) in the DR group were evaluated. The primary outcome was the retear rate, assessed by magnetic resonance imaging of the shoulder at 2-year follow-up. The clinical evaluation was based on the modified University of California, Los Angeles (UCLA) shoulder score and range of motion (ROM) measurements.

RESULTS: Magnetic resonance arthrography showed a lower full-thickness retear rate for the DR group than for the SR group (8% vs 24%, respectively; P < .05). At both 6-month and 2-year follow-up, there was no statistically significant difference in terms of the rate of stiffness in the SR and DR groups (8% vs 12% and 0% vs 0%, respectively; P > .05). No clinical differences were recorded regarding the UCLA score (SR group: mean, 32.6 [range, 30-35]; DR group: mean, 33.3 [range, 29-35]; P = .61) and ROM.

CONCLUSION: The treatment with double-row repair was not more effective than single-row repair in patients with rotator cuff tears in terms of clinical outcomes.
plain_language_summary:
Here's a summary of the passage that a fifth grader can understand:

The researchers were studying a type of surgery to fix a common injury called a rotator cuff tear. This injury happens when the muscles and tendons around the shoulder joint get hurt.

The researchers wanted to know if a new way of doing the surgery, called "double-row repair," was better than the old way, called "single-row repair." They wanted to see if the new way would help people's shoulders heal better and move more easily.

They did an experiment where they divided 58 people into two groups. One group got the old way of surgery, and the other group got the new way. Then, they checked on the people two years later to see how their shoulders were doing.

What they found out was that the new way of surgery was a little better at fixing the tear, but it didn't make a big difference in how well people's shoulders moved or felt. So, the researchers concluded that the new way of surgery wasn't really better than the old way.

That's the main idea of the passage!

Title: Double-Row Repair Lowers the Retear Risk After Accelerated Rehabilitation.
Journal: The American Journal of Sports Medicine
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim benefit based on (+) secondary endpoint
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Recently, an accelerated rehabilitation protocol after rotator cuff (RC) repair has been proposed for patients at risk of postoperative stiffness.

PURPOSE: To investigate, in patients undergoing early accelerated mobilization, whether double-row (DR) repair provides better clinical outcomes and a lower retear rate compared with single-row (SR) configurations.

STUDY DESIGN: Randomized controlled trial; Level of evidence, 1.

METHODS: A total of 58 patients were randomized to undergo either SR or DR repair. After 2 years of follow-up, 12 men and 13 women (mean age, 61.8 years; range, 52-67 years) in the SR group and 15 men and 10 women (mean age, 58.9 years; range, 51-69 years) in the DR group were evaluated. To assess the retear rate, magnetic resonance imaging of the shoulder was performed at 2-year follow-up. The clinical evaluation was based on the modified University of California, Los Angeles (UCLA) shoulder score and range of motion (ROM) measurements.

RESULTS: Magnetic resonance arthrography showed a significantly lower full-thickness retear rate for the DR group than for the SR group (8% vs 24%, respectively; P < .05). Conversely, at both 6-month and 2-year follow-up, there was no statistically significant difference in terms of the rate of stiffness in the SR and DR groups (8% vs 12% and 0% vs 0%, respectively; P > .05). No clinical differences were recorded regarding the UCLA score (SR group: mean, 32.6 [range, 30-35]; DR group: mean, 33.3 [range, 29-35]; P = .61) and ROM.

CONCLUSION: In selected patients at a high risk of shoulder stiffness and therefore necessitating accelerated postoperative rehabilitation, DR repair of the RC could lower retear rates.
plain_language_summary:
Here's a summary of the passage that a fifth grader can understand:

The passage is talking about a type of surgery to fix a problem with the shoulder called a "rotator cuff repair". The surgery is to help people who might get stiff shoulders after the operation.

The researchers wanted to see if a certain way of doing the surgery (called "double-row" repair) is better than another way (called "single-row" repair). They wanted to know if the double-row way would help people's shoulders feel better and not get hurt again.

They did an experiment with 58 people who had the surgery. They looked at how well the people's shoulders were doing after 2 years. They took special pictures of the shoulders to see if they were healing well.

What they found out was that the double-row way of doing the surgery helped people's shoulders heal better and not get hurt again as much as the single-row way. But, both ways of doing the surgery seemed to help people's shoulders feel about the same in terms of how well they could move and how much pain they had.

So, the researchers think that the double-row way of doing the surgery might be a good choice for people who are at risk of getting stiff shoulders after the operation.

Title: Dietary fat restriction increases fat taste sensitivity in people with obesity.
Spin in Abstract Title/Results: RESULTS: Focus on (+) secondary endpoint, RESULTS: "Trend toward significance" // "Numerically longer survival" or similar verbiage
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: "Trend toward significance" // "Numerically longer survival" or equivalent verbiage
abstract:
Objective: This study assessed the effect of a 6-week low-fat (LF) or portion control (PC) diet matched for weight loss on fat taste thresholds in people with overweight/obesity.

Methods: Participants (n = 53) completed a randomized dietary intervention and consumed either a LF diet (25% fat) or PC diet (33% fat) for 6 weeks. Fat taste thresholds (lowest detectable fat concentration) were assessed at baseline and week 6.

Results: Consumption of a LF diet (n = 26) and PC diet (n = 27) reduced participants' weight, with no significant differences between groups (LF, -2.9%, PC, -2.7%). Both diets resulted in a decrease in fat taste thresholds. The ability to perceive different fat concentrations in foods was increased after the LF diet only; however, food preference did not change on either diet.

Conclusions: The treatment with a low-fat diet was not more effective than a portion control diet in patients with overweight/obesity in increasing fat taste sensitivity.
plain_language_summary:
Here's a summary of the passage that a fifth grader can understand:

Scientists wanted to see if eating a special diet for 6 weeks would help people taste fat in food better. They took 53 people who were overweight and divided them into two groups. One group ate a diet with very little fat (like 25% of their daily calories), and the other group ate a diet where they controlled how much food they ate, but it had a bit more fat (like 33% of their daily calories).

After 6 weeks, both groups lost some weight, which is good! But the scientists were really looking at how well people could taste fat in food. They found out that both diets helped people taste fat a little better, but the low-fat diet helped people taste different amounts of fat in food even better. However, neither diet changed what foods people liked or disliked.

So, the main point is that eating a low-fat diet or a portion control diet can both help people taste fat in food a little better, but they're pretty equal in how well they work.

Title: Dietary fat restriction increases fat taste sensitivity in people with obesity.
Spin in Abstract Title/Results: RESULTS: Focus on (+) secondary endpoint, RESULTS: "Trend toward significance" // "Numerically longer survival" or similar verbiage
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: "Trend toward significance" // "Numerically longer survival" or equivalent verbiage
abstract:
Objective: Individuals with obesity may be less sensitive to the taste of fat, and it is hypothesized that this is due to excess dietary fat intake. This study assessed the effect of a 6-week low-fat (LF) or portion control (PC) diet matched for weight loss on fat taste thresholds, fat perception, and preference in people with overweight/obesity.

Methods: Participants (n = 53) completed a randomized dietary intervention and consumed either a LF diet (25% fat) or PC diet (33% fat) for 6 weeks. Fat taste thresholds (lowest detectable fat concentration), fat perception (discrimination ability), preference, and anthropometry were assessed at baseline and week 6.

Results: Consumption of a LF diet (n = 26) and PC diet (n = 27) reduced participants' weight (P < 0.001), with no significant differences between groups (LF, -2.9%, PC, -2.7%). Both diets resulted in a decrease in fat taste thresholds (P = 0.014), and the effect tended to be stronger in the LF diet vs. the PC diet (P = 0.060). The ability to perceive different fat concentrations in foods was increased after the LF diet only (P = 0.017); however, food preference did not change on either diet.

Conclusions: A PC and LF diet both increase fat taste sensitivity in people with overweight/obesity, with the strongest effect after the LF diet.
plain_language_summary:
Here's a summary of the passage that a fifth grader can understand:

The researchers wanted to know if people who are overweight or obese (meaning they are heavier than they should be) can taste fat in food as well as people who are not overweight. They thought that maybe people who eat a lot of fatty foods can't taste the fat as well because their taste buds get used to it.

To test this, they had 53 people eat a special diet for 6 weeks. Half of the people ate a diet with less fat (called the "low-fat" diet), and the other half ate a diet where they just ate smaller portions of food (called the "portion control" diet). Both diets helped people lose weight, which is good!

The researchers found out that both diets helped people taste fat better, but the low-fat diet helped even more. This means that when people eat less fat, their taste buds can detect fat in food better. But, it didn't change what foods people liked or disliked.

So, the main idea is that eating less fat can help people taste fat better, which might help them make healthier food choices.

Title: A randomized controlled clinical and radiological trial about outcomes of navigation-assisted TKA compared to conventional TKA: long-term follow-up.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim benefit based on (+) secondary endpoint
abstract:
PURPOSE: This prospective randomized study compared navigation-assisted total knee arthroplasty (TKA) and conventional TKA, reporting clinical and radiological outcomes at a follow-up of 9 years. The purpose of this study was to compare the clinical and radiological outcomes for patients who underwent navigation-assisted TKA or conventional TKA after long-term follow-up.

METHODS: A total of 80 patients (88 knees) were available for physical and radiological examination 9 years after TKA. Clinical outcomes were evaluated using HSS, WOMAC, and KS function and pain scores. Radiological outcomes of component loosening and its survivorship during the 9-year follow-up were also evaluated.

RESULTS: There were no significant differences in clinical outcomes between the two groups. In terms of radiological outcomes, the navigation group had fewer alignment outliers (7.3% vs 20%, p = 0.006). The survival rate was slightly better in the navigation group than in the conventional group without statistical significance (best-case scenario 100% vs 95.3%, n.s., worst-case scenario 95.6% vs 88.4%, n.s.).

CONCLUSION: The treatment with navigation-assisted TKA was not more effective than conventional TKA in patients with total knee arthroplasty regarding clinical outcomes.
plain_language_summary:
Here's a summary of the passage that a fifth grader can understand:

The researchers did a study to compare two ways of doing a special kind of surgery called total knee arthroplasty (TKA). This surgery helps people who have bad knees.

They wanted to see if using a special tool called "navigation" during the surgery made a difference in how well the surgery worked. They looked at two groups of people: one group had the surgery with the navigation tool, and the other group had the surgery without it.

They checked on the people 9 years after the surgery to see how they were doing. They looked at how well the people could move their knees, how much pain they had, and if the surgery had worked well.

What they found out was that both groups of people were doing about the same in terms of moving their knees and having pain. But, the group that had the navigation tool during surgery had fewer problems with the way their knee was aligned (meaning, the knee was put together correctly).

So, the researchers concluded that using the navigation tool during surgery didn't make a big difference in how well the people did after 9 years. Both ways of doing the surgery seemed to work about the same.

Does that make sense?

Title: A randomized controlled clinical and radiological trial about outcomes of navigation-assisted TKA compared to conventional TKA: long-term follow-up.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim benefit based on (+) secondary endpoint
abstract:
PURPOSE: Well balanced knees with good alignment are essential for a well-functioning TKA with long survival of its implants. This prospective randomized study comparing navigation-assisted TKA and conventional TKA reported the clinical and radiological outcomes at a follow-up of 9В years. The purpose of this study was to compare the clinical and radiological outcomes for patients who underwent navigation-assisted TKA or conventional TKA after long-term follow-up.

METHODS: A total of 80 patients (88 knees) were available for physical and radiological examination 9В years after TKA. Clinical outcomes were evaluated using HSS, WOMAC, and KS function and pain scores. And radiological outcomes of the component loosening and its survivorship during 9-year follow-up were also evaluated.

RESULTS: There were no significant differences in the field of clinical outcomes between the two groups. In terms of radiological outcomes, the navigation group had fewer alignment outliers (7.3 vs 20В %, pВ =В 0.006). Although the clinical outcomes showed no differences between the two groups, the survival rate was slightly better in the navigation group than in the conventional group without statistical significance (best-case scenario 100 vs 95.3В %, n.s., worst-case scenario 95.6 vs 88.4В %, n.s.).

CONCLUSION: Navigation-assisted TKA produced better alignment outcomes and better survival rates than conventional instruments although some of the differences were not statistically significant.

I'd be happy to help you explain this passage to your fifth grader.

This passage is talking about a special kind of surgery called a Total Knee Arthroplasty (TKA). It's when doctors replace a person's knee joint with an artificial one.

The researchers wanted to see if using a special tool called "navigation" during the surgery made a difference in how well the new knee worked and how long it lasted. They compared two groups of people: one group had the surgery with the navigation tool, and the other group had the surgery without it.

They looked at how well the people's knees were working 9 years after the surgery. They found out that:

* Both groups had similar results in terms of how well their knees felt and functioned.
* But, the group that had the navigation tool during surgery had fewer problems with the alignment of their new knee joint. (Think of it like making sure the knee is properly lined up, like a puzzle piece.)
* The navigation group also had a slightly better chance of their new knee joint lasting longer, but it wasn't a huge difference.

So, the researchers concluded that using the navigation tool during surgery might be a little better for getting the knee joint aligned properly and making it last longer, but more research is needed to be sure.

I hope that helps your fifth grader understand the passage!

Title: Is There a Benefit for Liposomal Bupivacaine Compared to a Traditional Periarticular Injection in Total Knee Arthroplasty Patients With a History of Chronic Opioid Use?
abstract:
Background: Postoperative pain after total knee arthroplasty (TKA) poses a major challenge. It delays mobilization, increases opioid consumption and side effects, and lengthens hospitalization. This challenge multiplies when treating an opioid-dependent population. We examined whether a novel suspended release local anesthetic, liposomal bupivacaine (LB) would improve pain control and decrease opioid consumption after TKA compared to a standard periarticular injection in opioid-dependent patients.

Methods: Thirty-eight patients undergoing TKA were randomly assigned to receive either a periarticular injection (PAI) with LB (n = 20) or with a standard PAI (including a combination of ropivacaine, clonidine, Toradol, Epinepherine, and saline; n = 18) as part of a multimodal pain management approach. All periarticular injections were done by a single surgeon. Perioperative treatment was similar between groups. Postoperative information regarding pain level was evaluated by a pain visual analog scale score. Postoperative opioid consumption was recorded.

Results: After controlling baseline narcotic usage before surgery, no differences were found between groups in daily postoperative narcotic usage (P = .113), average daily pain score (P = .332), or maximum daily pain score (P = .881). However, when examining pain levels separately for each day, pain visual analog scale scores were reported higher in post operative day 1 in the LB group (P = .033).

Conclusions: LB was not found to be superior to standard PAI in opioid-dependent patients undergoing TKA. This patient population continues to present a challenge even with modern multimodal pain protocols.
plain_language_summary:
Here's a summary of the passage that a fifth grader can understand:

Doctors were trying to find a better way to help people who have knee replacement surgery feel less pain after the operation. They wanted to see if a new kind of medicine called liposomal bupivacaine (LB) would work better than the usual medicine they use.

They tested the new medicine on 38 people who had knee replacement surgery. Half of the people got the new medicine, and the other half got the usual medicine. The doctors then asked the patients how much pain they were feeling and how much pain medicine they needed to take.

What they found out was that the new medicine didn't work any better than the usual medicine. The people who got the new medicine didn't have less pain or need less pain medicine than the people who got the usual medicine. In fact, the people who got the new medicine actually felt a little more pain on the first day after surgery.

So, the doctors learned that the new medicine isn't a better solution for helping people with knee replacement surgery feel less pain. They still need to find a better way to help people who are already taking pain medicine before they have surgery.

Title: Is There a Benefit for Liposomal Bupivacaine Compared to a Traditional Periarticular Injection in Total Knee Arthroplasty Patients With a History of Chronic Opioid Use?
abstract:
Background: Postoperative pain after total knee arthroplasty (TKA) poses a major challenge. It delays mobilization, increases opioid consumption and side effects, and lengthens hospitalization. We examined whether a novel suspended release local anesthetic, liposomal bupivacaine (LB), would improve pain control and decrease opioid consumption after TKA compared to a standard periarticular injection.

Methods: Thirty-eight patients undergoing TKA were randomly assigned to receive either a periarticular injection (PAI) with LB (n = 20) or with a standard PAI (including a combination of ropivacaine, clonidine, Toradol, Epinephrine, and saline; n = 18) as part of a multimodal pain management approach. All periarticular injections were done by a single surgeon. Perioperative treatment was similar between groups. The primary outcome was postoperative pain level evaluated by a pain visual analog scale score. Postoperative opioid consumption was recorded.

Results: No differences were found between groups in daily postoperative narcotic usage (P = .113), average daily pain score (P = .332), or maximum daily pain score (P = .881).

Conclusions: LB was not more effective than standard PAI in patients undergoing TKA.
plain_language_summary:
Here's a summary of the passage that a fifth grader can understand:

Doctors were trying to find a better way to help people feel less pain after a big surgery on their knee. They wanted to see if a new kind of medicine would work better than the usual medicine they use.

They tested the new medicine on 38 people who had the surgery. Half of them got the new medicine, and the other half got the usual medicine. They wanted to see who would feel less pain and need fewer pain pills.

But, it turned out that the new medicine didn't work any better than the usual medicine. Both groups of people felt about the same amount of pain, and they needed about the same number of pain pills.

So, the doctors learned that the new medicine isn't a better choice than the usual medicine for helping people feel less pain after knee surgery.

Title: Conventional En Masse Repair Versus Separate Double-Layer Double-Row Repair for the Treatment of Delaminated Rotator Cuff Tears.
Journal: The American Journal of Sports Medicine
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim equivalence/non-inferiority versus control for a (-) endpoint
abstract:
BACKGROUND: The rotator cuff tendon is known to exert a shear force between the superficial and deep layers. Owing to this characteristic, separate repair of delaminated rotator cuff tears has been introduced for the restoration of the physiological biomechanics of the rotator cuff. However, whether conventional en masse repair or separate repair is superior is controversial in terms of outcomes.

PURPOSE: To compare clinical outcomes between conventional en masse repair and separate double-layer double-row repair for the treatment of delaminated rotator cuff tears.

STUDY DESIGN: Randomized controlled study; Level of evidence, 2.

METHODS: Between August 2007 and March 2014, a total of 82 patients who underwent arthroscopic rotator cuff repair of a delaminated tear were enrolled and randomized into 2 groups. In group 1 (n = 48), arthroscopic conventional en masse repair was performed. In group 2 (n = 34), separate double-layer double-row repair was performed. The primary outcome was the American Shoulder and Elbow Surgeons score. The Constant score, Simple Shoulder Test score, and visual analog scale (VAS) score for pain and range of motion (ROM) were also assessed before surgery; at 3, 6, and 12 months after surgery; and at the last follow-up visit. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was performed at 12 months postoperatively to examine the retear rate and pattern.

RESULTS: There was no significant difference between groups in the preoperative demographic data, including patient age, sex, symptom duration, tear size, and functional scores (P > .05). The mean follow-up period was 25.9 ± 1.2 months. No significant differences in functional scores and ROM were found between the 2 groups at each time point. Eight (17%) of 48 patients in group 1 and 6 (18%) of 34 patients in group 2 showed retears on MRI at 12-month follow-up (P > .05).

CONCLUSION: The treatment with conventional en masse repair was not more effective than separate double-layer double-row repair in patients with delaminated rotator cuff tears.
plain_language_summary:
I'd be happy to help you explain this passage to your fifth grader.

This passage is about a study that compared two ways to fix a type of injury called a "rotator cuff tear." The rotator cuff is a group of muscles and tendons that help your shoulder move.

Imagine you have a big rubber band that helps you lift your arm. If that rubber band gets torn, it can be very painful and hard to move your arm. Doctors can fix this tear by sewing it back together. But there are two ways to do this: one way is to sew the whole tear together at once, and the other way is to sew it together in two layers.

The study wanted to see which way is better. They looked at 82 people who had this type of injury and divided them into two groups. One group got the "whole tear" fix, and the other group got the "two-layer" fix. Then, they checked how well each group was doing after the surgery.

What they found out was that both ways of fixing the tear worked about the same. The people in both groups were able to move their arms and shoulders about the same, and they had about the same amount of pain. So, the study concluded that it doesn't really matter which way you fix the tear - both ways are good!

Does that make sense to you and your fifth grader?

Title: Conventional En Masse Repair Versus Separate Double-Layer Double-Row Repair for the Treatment of Delaminated Rotator Cuff Tears.
Journal: The American Journal of Sports Medicine
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim equivalence/non-inferiority versus control for a (-) endpoint
abstract:
BACKGROUND: The rotator cuff tendon is known to exert a shear force between the superficial and deep layers. Owing to this characteristic, separate repair of delaminated rotator cuff tears has been introduced for the restoration of the physiological biomechanics of the rotator cuff. However, whether conventional en masse repair or separate repair is superior is controversial in terms of outcomes.

PURPOSE: To compare clinical outcomes between conventional en masse repair and separate double-layer double-row repair for the treatment of delaminated rotator cuff tears.

STUDY DESIGN: Randomized controlled study; Level of evidence, 2.

METHODS: Between August 2007 and March 2014, a total of 82 patients who underwent arthroscopic rotator cuff repair of a delaminated tear were enrolled and randomized into 2 groups. In group 1 (n = 48), arthroscopic conventional en masse repair was performed. In group 2 (n = 34), separate double-layer double-row repair was performed. The American Shoulder and Elbow Surgeons score, Constant score, Simple Shoulder Test score, and visual analog scale (VAS) score for pain and range of motion (ROM) were assessed before surgery; at 3, 6, and 12 months after surgery; and at the last follow-up visit. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was performed at 12 months postoperatively to examine the retear rate and pattern.

RESULTS: There was no significant difference between groups in the preoperative demographic data, including patient age, sex, symptom duration, tear size, and functional scores (P > .05). The mean follow-up period was 25.9 В± 1.2 months. Significant improvements in functional and pain scores were observed in both groups at the last follow-up visit. However, no significant differences in functional scores and ROM were found between the 2 groups at each time point, except that group 2 had significantly lower VAS pain scores (P < .05) at 3, 6, and 12 months postoperatively. Eight (17%) of 48 patients in group 1 and 6 (18%) of 34 patients in group 2 showed retears on MRI at 12-month follow-up (P > .05).

CONCLUSION: Both conventional en masse repair and separate double-layer double-row repair were effective in improving clinical outcomes in the treatment of delaminated rotator cuff tears. Lower pain scores were seen in patients who underwent separate double-layer double-row repair.
plain_language_summary:
Here's a summary of the passage that a fifth grader can understand:

The rotator cuff is a group of muscles and tendons in the shoulder that help us move our arm. Sometimes, these tendons can get torn, which can be very painful. Doctors have two ways to fix these tears: one way is to repair the whole tendon at once, and the other way is to repair the tendon in two layers.

The researchers wanted to know which way is better, so they did a study. They took 82 people who had torn rotator cuffs and divided them into two groups. One group got the whole tendon repaired at once, and the other group got the two-layer repair.

The researchers then checked on the people in both groups to see how they were doing. They asked them questions about how much pain they were in, how well they could move their arm, and how happy they were with their shoulder. They also took pictures of the shoulder with a special machine called an MRI to see if the tear had healed.

What they found out was that both ways of repairing the tendon worked well, and people in both groups were happy with their results. But, the people who got the two-layer repair had less pain than the people who got the whole tendon repaired at once.

So, the researchers concluded that both ways of repairing the tendon are good, but the two-layer repair might be a little better because it helps with pain.

Title: Comparison of Treatments for Superior Labrum-Biceps Complex Lesions With Concomitant Rotator Cuff Repair: A Prospective, Randomized, Comparative Analysis of Debridement, Biceps Tenotomy, and Biceps Tenodesis.
Journal: Arthroscopy: The Journal of Arthroscopic & Related Surgery
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: "Trend toward significance" // "Numerically longer survival" or equivalent verbiage, Claim benefit based on (+) subgroup analysis
abstract:
PURPOSE: To compare the clinical outcomes in patients with concomitant superior labrum-biceps complex (SLBC) lesions and rotator cuff tears who underwent arthroscopic rotator cuff repair, according to 3 different treatment methods (simple debridement, biceps tenotomy, or biceps tenodesis) for the SLBC lesions.

METHODS: One hundred twenty patients who underwent arthroscopic rotator cuff repair with SLBC lesions (biceps partial tears <50%, partial pulley lesions, and type II SLAP lesions) were enrolled in this prospective comparative study and randomly assigned to 1 of 3 treatment groups (simple debridement [Deb], biceps tenotomy only [BTo], or biceps tenodesis with one suture anchor [BTd]). Patients with isolated subscapularis tears or osteoarthritis were excluded. Finally, 86 patients (Deb in 28, BTo in 27, and BTd in 31) were analyzed (mean follow-up, 22.1 ± 7.72 months; mean age, 58.98 ± 7.8 years). The primary outcome was pain. Functional, clinical, and radiologic outcomes, as well as the strength index of elbow flexion and forearm supination, were also analyzed.

RESULTS: Pain, range of motion, and functional scores improved postoperatively in all 3 groups, with no significant differences across groups. At the 6-month follow-up, 8 patients reported cramping pain (5 Deb patients, 2 BTo patients, and 1 BTd patient). Bicipital groove tenderness improved in the BTo group postoperatively (P = .006). The Popeye deformity was noted in 10 patients (37.0%) in the BTo group, 8 (25.8%) in the BTd group, and 2 (7.1%) in the Deb group (P = .029). On radiologic examination, the presence of tenodesis of the biceps tendons on the bicipital groove showed low agreement with the presence of the Popeye deformity (observed in 13 of 20 patients with the deformity, 65%). On strength index analysis, the BTo group showed lower forearm supination strength (0.877 ± 0.44) compared with that in the other groups (1.29 ± 0.525 in the Deb group and 1.12 ± 0.451 in the BTd group, P = .049).CONCLUSIONS: The treatment methods did not show significant differences in improving pain and function in patients with SLBC lesions and rotator cuff tears. The treatment A was not more effective than comparator B in patients with these conditions.
plain_language_summary:
Here's a summary of the passage in plain language that a fifth grader can understand:

The researchers wanted to see which of three different ways to fix a certain kind of injury to the shoulder was best. The injury is called a "superior labrum-biceps complex lesion" and it happens when the tendons and ligaments in the shoulder get hurt.

They looked at 120 people who had this kind of injury and divided them into three groups. Each group got a different kind of treatment to fix the injury. The treatments were:

* Just cleaning up the area around the injury (called "debridement")
* Cutting the tendon that connects the bicep muscle to the shoulder bone (called "tenotomy")
* Attaching the tendon to the shoulder bone with a special anchor (called "tenodesis")

The researchers then looked at how well each group did after the treatment. They wanted to know if the people had less pain, if they could move their shoulders better, and if they were stronger.

What they found out was that all three treatments helped people get better, but none of them was significantly better than the others. Some people had some side effects, like cramping pain or a weird bump on their arm (called a "Popeye deformity"), but overall, the treatments were all about the same.

So, the researchers concluded that it doesn't really matter which of the three treatments you get, because they all seem to work about the same.

Finally, 86 patients (Deb in 28, BTo in 27, and BTd in 31) were analyzed (mean follow-up, 22.1 В± 7.72 months; mean age, 58.98 В± 7.8 years). Pain; functional, clinical, and radiologic outcomes; and the strength index of elbow flexion and forearm supination were analyzed.

RESULTS: Pain, range of motion, and functional scores significantly improved postoperatively in all 3 groups, with no significant differences across groups. At the 6-month follow-up, 8 patients reported cramping pain (5 Deb patients, 2 BTo patients, and 1 BTd patient), but the pain improved in most patients over time (with cramping pain reported by zero Deb patients, 1 BTo patient, and 1 BTd patient at final follow-up). Bicipital groove tenderness significantly improved in the BTo group postoperatively (PВ = .006). The Popeye deformity was noted in 10 patients (37.0%) in the BTo group, 8 (25.8%) in the BTd group, and only 2 (7.1%) in the Deb group (PВ = .029). On radiologic examination, the presence of tenodesis of the biceps tendons on the bicipital groove showed low agreement with the presence of the Popeye deformity (observed in 13 of 20 patients with the deformity, 65%). On strength index analysis, the BTo group showed lower forearm supination strength (0.877 В± 0.44) compared with that in the other groups (1.29 В± 0.525 in the Deb group and 1.12 В± 0.451 in the BTd group, PВ = .049).CONCLUSIONS: All 3 treatments improved pain and function. Simple debridement showed the lowest risk of the Popeye deformity and preserved forearm supination strength. Biceps tenotomy and tenodesis may be preferable for selected patients: biceps tenotomy for patients with definite bicipital groove tenderness and biceps tenodesis for patients, especially male patients, with bicipital groove tenderness who want to preserve supination strength.

Here's a summary of the passage in plain language that a fifth grader can understand:

They looked at 120 people who had this problem and divided them into three groups. Each group got a different treatment to fix the problem. The treatments were:

1. Just cleaning up the area (like wiping away dirt)
2. Cutting a tendon (like a strong string) that connects the bicep muscle to the shoulder
3. Attaching the tendon to the shoulder bone with a special anchor

The researchers then checked how the people were doing after the treatment. They looked at how much pain they had, how well they could move their shoulder, and how strong their arm was.

What they found out was that all three treatments helped people feel better and move their shoulder more easily. But, there were some differences between the groups. The group that just got their area cleaned up had fewer problems with their bicep muscle looking weird (called the "Popeye deformity"). The group that got their tendon cut had more problems with their bicep muscle looking weird, but they also had less pain in their shoulder. The group that got their tendon attached to the bone had a mix of both good

Title: Effects of a mindfulness-based weight loss intervention in adults with obesity: A randomized clinical trial.
abstract:
Objective: To determine whether adding mindfulness-based eating and stress management practices to a diet-exercise program improves weight loss.

Methods: In this study, 194 adults with obesity were randomized to a 5.5-month program with or without mindfulness training and identical diet-exercise guidelines. Intention-to-treat analyses with multiple imputation were used for missing data. The primary outcome was 18-month weight change.

Results: Estimated effects comparing the mindfulness to control arm showed weight loss at 12 months, -1.9 kg (95% CI: -4.5, 0.8; P = 0.17), and 18 months, -1.7 kg (95% CI: -4.7, 1.2; P = 0.24), which were not statistically significant. Changes in fasting glucose at 12 months were -3.1 mg/dl (95% CI: -6.3, 0.1; P = 0.06), and 18 months, -4.1 mg/dl (95% CI: -7.3, -0.9; P = 0.01). Changes in triglyceride/HDL ratio at 12 months were -0.57 (95% CI: -0.95, -0.18; P = 0.004), and 18 months, -0.36 (95% CI: -0.74, 0.03; P = 0.07). Estimates for other metabolic risk factors, including waist circumference, blood pressure, and C-reactive protein, were not statistically significant.

Conclusions: The treatment with mindfulness was not more effective than the comparator in patients with obesity for weight change.
plain_language_summary:
Here's a summary of the passage that a fifth grader can understand:

Scientists wanted to see if teaching people special ways to eat and manage stress would help them lose more weight. They took 194 adults who were overweight and divided them into two groups. One group got special training on how to eat mindfully (paying attention to their food and hunger) and managing stress, while the other group didn't get this training. Both groups were told to follow the same diet and exercise plan.

The scientists looked at how much weight each group lost after 12 months and 18 months. They also checked other health measures like blood sugar levels, cholesterol, and blood pressure.

What they found out was that the group that got the special training didn't lose more weight than the other group. However, the training did seem to help with some other health measures, like blood sugar levels and cholesterol.

So, the scientists concluded that adding mindfulness training to a diet and exercise plan didn't make a big difference in how much weight people lost. But it might have some other benefits for overall health.

Title: Effects of a mindfulness-based weight loss intervention in adults with obesity: A randomized clinical trial.
abstract:
Objective: To determine whether adding mindfulness-based eating and stress management practices to a diet-exercise program improves weight loss and metabolic syndrome components.

Methods: In this study 194 adults with obesity were randomized to a 5.5-month program with or without mindfulness training and identical diet-exercise guidelines. Intention-to-treat analyses with multiple imputation were used for missing data. The primary outcome was 18-month weight change.

Results: Estimated effects comparing the mindfulness to control arm favored the mindfulness arm in (a) weight loss at 12 months, -1.9 kg (95% CI: -4.5, 0.8; P = 0.17), and 18 months, -1.7 kg (95% CI: -4.7, 1.2; P = 0.24), though not statistically significant; (b) changes in fasting glucose at 12 months, -3.1 mg/dl (95% CI: -6.3, 0.1; P = 0.06), and 18 months, -4.1 mg/dl (95% CI: -7.3, -0.9; P = 0.01); and (c) changes in triglyceride/HDL ratio at 12 months, -0.57 (95% CI: -0.95, -0.18; P = 0.004), and 18 months, -0.36 (95% CI: -0.74, 0.03; P = 0.07). Estimates for other metabolic risk factors were not statistically significant, including waist circumference, blood pressure, and C-reactive protein.

Conclusions: Mindfulness enhancements to a diet-exercise program did not show substantial weight loss benefit but may promote long-term improvement in some aspects of metabolic health in obesity that requires further study.

Here's a summary of the passage that a fifth grader can understand:
